content:
3 min read
Just now

--

AI is not the biggest question. The question is what we become when we use it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Humanity has never had access to so much knowledge.
A question that once required hours in a library can now be answered in seconds.
A single person can reach more information than entire generations before them could have imagined.
And yet something strange is happening.
Many people do not feel clearer.
They feel more lost.
We often think the problem of our time is a lack of information.
But maybe the real problem lies elsewhere.
Maybe we are surrounded by answers while slowly losing our ability to know which questions truly matter.
Every day, invisible systems compete for our attention.
They suggest what to watch.
What to buy.
Who to admire.
What success should look like.
What we should become.
And this is the subtle trap:
these systems do not simply leave us without direction.
They give us one.
Constantly.
Effortlessly.
Without friction.
There is always another video, another recommendation, another model to follow.
We rarely feel lost, because we are always being guided somewhere.
It is a manufactured direction that feels like a chosen one.
Little by little, without noticing, we begin living by external directions.
Not because someone forces us.
But because we stop asking:
“Is this really my direction?”
Or worse —
we stop noticing that the question even needs to be asked.
AI enters this world
Artificial intelligence is a remarkable instrument.
It can organize knowledge.
Explore possibilities.
Help us create.
Accelerate our thinking.
But acceleration creates a new responsibility.
A faster instrument does not automatically create a wiser human.
We can move faster in the wrong direction.
A powerful tool in the hands of someone without direction can amplify confusion as easily as it amplifies intelligence.
The instrument and the person
A guitar does not create music by itself.
The instrument carries possibilities.
The result depends on the person who holds it.
The same guitar can produce classical music, flamenco, rock, metal — or silence.
The instrument reveals possibilities.
The human gives them meaning.
But AI introduces something different.
A guitar does not play while you are still searching for the question.
It waits.
AI can answer before our own thought has finished forming.
It can fill the silence.
And sometimes, that silence was exactly where reflection and judgment were born.
The question beneath the question
Perhaps the central question of the AI era is not:
“What can AI do?”
The deeper question is:
“Who are we becoming while using it?”
The challenge is not to reject technology.
Nor to worship it.
The challenge is to rebuild the connection between human beings and their own direction.
Before asking an external system:
“What should I do?”
maybe we need to return to a more fundamental question:
“Who am I, and what truly matters to me?”
The future will not belong only to those who possess the most powerful tools.
It will belong to those who understand why they use them.
Because intelligence without direction becomes noise.
Information without understanding becomes confusion.
Speed without meaning becomes movement without purpose.
Maybe the greatest challenge of the coming years is not teaching machines to think.
Maybe it is remembering how humans choose.
Because every powerful instrument eventually asks the same question:
Who is holding it?
Question for reflection
Will AI help humans rediscover their ability to think and choose —
or will it slowly make thinking feel optional?
-Irkif Hillat
